sentences.text: Are you taking-- can I turn the chair a little bit?
This way?
OK, any time.
Yeah, that doesn't matter.
My name is Daniel Geslewitz.
D-A-N-I-E-L G-E-S-L-E-W-I-T-Z.
That was Gedalia Geclewicz.
G-E-D-A-L-I-A G-E-C-L-E-W-I-C-Z.
I was born in Lodz, Poland.
L-O-D-Z.
I was born on August the 14, 1924.
Now I'm 71, going to be 72.
Thanks.
Yeah, I can tell you about family.
I was the youngest of seven children.
My father had his own business.
He was in the cabinet-makers-- cabinet-making business.
All my brothers worked for my father.
Was-- I was going to school, attending public school.
I had seven years of public school.
That's all what was compulsory.
And after the seven years, you were out, and you-- that was the end of it.

But I used to attend technical school, evening, because as a-- if you learned a trade, that the law required that you work so many hours, and then you-- after noon you have to attend technical school.
And this was till the war broke out.
There was no more-- no more education for Jewish kids, any more.
I was 15 years old.
Yeah, at that time, I was.
The war broke out, it was vacation time.
But, once this-- after vacation, they-- I went to school, and there was an assembly.
And the principal, with a friend of mine, made me get up and leave the-- leave the assembly, because he said there no more education for Jewish kids.
As far as a Jewish home, we were strictly traditional religious.
We had a Jewish kashrut, was strictly kashrut.
We observed all the Jewish holidays, all the Jewish holidays.
Yom Kippur.

First, Saturday, the most-- best day was Friday night, where the whole family used to gather around the table, and there was a festive meal, and-- and the candles, and the kiddush used to make the wine.
And was-- the light was bright and was very, very heartwarming, where looking back, it sort of was-- every Friday was a gathering of the family.
And no business, no nothing, and it was-- and left good memories.
Yeah, food that we could not eat the whole week, but on Friday we had food like chicken soup, and fish, and all kind of stuff that usually you did not get during the weekday.
My part?
My part was going with my mother to the market and help her carry the-- the basket with food.
We didn't have no refrigeration, so everything had to be bought fresh.
So I helped to carry the basket, whatever she would-- chicken, you know, live chicken and live fish.

And then she had to go to the guy-- to the shokhet, and-- and the chicken had to be killed, you know, according to the-- it was prescribed by Jewish law, and then was the fish, and you prepared-- she worked the whole day.
Just prepare the Friday and Shabbat meal, because Shabbat-- we didn't cook on Sabbath.
So she had-- she had some job on her hand to feed nine people.
For two days.
Yeah.
Yom-- Yom Kippur was a special, a solemn day.
It was kind of hard on a kid, you know, because we couldn't eat.
When I grew up, you had to fast, and you couldn't-- you-- you had a hard time, you know, waiting until you got to eat again.
And-- but and then after the-- the services, the whole family got together.
We broke fast together.
And, you know, that was a solemn day.
Passover was a good-- Passover was a very welcome holiday, because we got new clothes.
We got new shoes, and we got whatever new stuff, and-- which we didn't get often.
New-- new suit, new shoe, new shirt, and you're dressed up, you look like a bright new nickel.
And so we were-- so Passover was kind of a welcome holiday, because your-- the expectation to get material things, like clothes, and all sorts of stuff.
We prepared it-- pardon me?
My part was to do anything my mother asked me.
There was-- there was a lot to do.
There was go to fetch the matzohs, and-- and keep them separate.
And there was a whole operation, Passover, because everything had to be clean.
And everything was turned upside down, you know, and-- and prepare food, different food, and-- but somehow, you know, it worked out.
Everything, you know, hard work, but when Passover came, and the-- the Seder came, everything was festive and-- and, you know, relaxed.
So it was a memorable day for me as a youngster.
I know you didn't go to the supermarket.
Where--
No.
The matzoh was-- bakeries, and they were baking matzoh.
So you had to go to the bakery and-- and take-- it was not packed like here, you go and get the box of matzoh.
So you took a cover-- I remember from-- from pillow cover, because it's clean, had to be clean.
And they used to fill it up, and I used to carry it home on my back, because we didn't have any cars, or something like that.
So me being the young-- the youngest, and the others had to work, so that was my job, you know, to bring the matzoh home.
That was one of my duties.
And help out, and whatever I was asked to do, I had to do.
What do you remember from the Seder?
From the Seder was-- the whole family got together, again, and was festive.
And it was like renewal.
Everything was new clothes, everybody dressed up in their best garments.
And-- and-- and the candlelight, the candles lit, and the-- and everybody sitting around, like they sit around the table making kiddush.
And-- and-- and it gave you a warm feeling.
There was only family.
Only family was at the Seder.
No, no.
There were no grandparents.
My grandparents-- my father came to Lodz from a small town, from a little village in-- in [?
Koretz, ?] [?
Alishnegetz, ?] I guess it was, or something like that.
I don't know exactly.
But there was no grandparents.
Only was immediate family.
Well, I remember Shavuos.
I remember all the holidays.
Sukkos and Shavuos was a nice holiday, because the weather was nice, and you were not so restricted.
And there was Sukkos.
You-- you-- you have to build a sukkah, and that was my job, helping build a sukkah.
In the back-- in the front yard.
In front of the house.
Yeah, of course.
It was a building.
It was a building, and it was like communal sukkah, for-- for-- for more Jewish families, you know, the-- only mostly the-- the parents used to go and eat in the sukkah, the children didn't go.
You know, the brothers.
And then, other families, the head of the families used to go and use the sukkah for the same purpose.
Yeah.
One or two, it depends.
I don't remember.
But one, I know for sure.
I know it was a bigger one, because it served for the whole building, for all the Jewish families.
Occasionally.
Yeah.
Every-- every-- every Saturday.
Never-- no fail.
And no fail, every Saturday, he went to shul, and Friday night.
No.
No.
He-- he-- [?
European. ?]
He wore-- he wore-- he-- his-- he dressed like very nontraditional, I would say.
He wasn't-- he didn't have no beard.
He-- I remember he had a mustache, yes, but no beard.
You couldn't distinguish him between-- but he was-- we were religious and observant.
As far as the Jewish-- what was required, but you were preparing food.
Yeah.
There was-- Lodz as a whole, or-- or just neighborhood?
Lodz, I'll tell you a little bit about Lodz.
Lodz had about 600,000 people, and it was about half was Jewish.
Half of the 600,000 were Jews.
And where we lived were mixed, mostly Jewish people, in our building.
There was some-- a German family.
The-- the landlord was German.
He lived in the same building.
And there were other Polish families, but the majority were Jewish.
The we-- we lived was a mix, but I attended a strictly Jewish school.
Public school was--
Like-- was Jewish.
And it was always a problem, because the animosity between the Polish people.
And they always used to-- walking home from school, they used to beat us up, or you know, or try to find-- and hit us, and so forth.
It was always a problem to-- to watch out for yourself, not to get into the wrong place, you know?
Because there always a danger that you will get hurt.
They-- they didn't kill you, but they beat you up.
So by living in the city, you knew what street to go to school and the time-- the time-- and they let out the schools at a certain time.
They let us out at a different time so we should not, you know, meet or something.
You should not-- you should separate them.
We always tried to separate ourselves from-- from-- from the Polish kids.
You mean that the different schools let the children out at different times?
Yeah.
When I was in school, we-- we let us out at the different time, because if they-- sometimes same time, there was always a danger of them ganging up and beating up the Jewish kids.
All Jewish kids.
Polish.
Polish.
Home.
We strictly-- home, we spoke Yiddish.
And between our friends, we spoke Yiddish too.
Yiddish was the main language, I would say.
And Polish, too, but we used Yiddish a lot.
Everybody spoke Yiddish.
In the street, yeah.
Everybody.
With friends, relatives, everybody.
In the marketplace.
At the workplace.
Pardon me?
You mean in the store, in the shop, in our work?
Yeah, we spoke Yiddish among ourselves.
Yeah.
There was a woodworking shop.
We used to build kitchen cabinets mostly, and-- and all my brothers worked for them.
And then he had some other additional, you know, people, painters and so forth.
Before me, yeah.
They all-- yeah, they all went to school.
I don't know exactly, but all went to school.
And then in order to become a journeyman, you had to have so many years technical school.
So, but I was the youngest, but they were already-- they were journeymen.
They were craftsmen, which I was not.
Were cabinetmakers.
Five.
Together, with me, five.
With you, five.
So the other four brothers had all been trained as craftsmen.
Yeah, cabinetmakers.
Yeah.
And the girls.
One was two years older, so she-- she was young, and-- but Chaya-- Helen-- she was a seamstress.
She was trained to be a seamstress.
My-- only my-- before the war, my sister got married, the older sister.
Helen got married.
And she had a boy.
Yosel.
And she had a boy, I guess at the beginning of the war, or something like this.
He-- he-- the beginning of the war.
Right at the beginning, or the start of the war.
In this period, I don't know exactly.
She had a boy, that was the only nephew I had.
Or grandchild.
She had a child.
Yeah.
Yeah.
She-- we all-- we all lived in Lodz.
There were-- because-- you see, there was no-- very hard to get higher education for Jewish people in Poland.
The schools-- the government schools-- they're limited, the amount of Jews.
Very-- very limited.
It was impossible to get into.
So when a child-- we finished public school, which was seven years, you had to learn a trade.
So you had lots of craftsmen.
So that was-- you had to go out in the world and learn so you would be able to sustain yourself.
That's why you had-- you had lots of craftsmen, and very few people, none of my friends, went to-- to high school.
Because it was practically impossible.
But you did go to Jewish school, you said.
You want to cheder.
Yeah, to cheder I went, which to learn about praying and, you know, learn the Jewish--
We learned how to-- it was one big room.
It was mostly one-- all the Jewish men with the beard, traditional.
We had lots of kids with the lots of noise, you know, and this, and he did the best he could to-- to teach us the Jewish alphabet and how to-- you know, to read from the siddur, from the book.
And the usual thing.
Praying, mostly praying.
But not-- not-- other-- they didn't educate us in this school, like mathematics, or-- or, you know, English, Polish, was strictly religious.
Fair.
Never had any-- no complaints.
I [? faired ?] better.
Otherwise, you know, it wasn't-- it was different.
That time you had to be very, you know, respectful of the teacher, and had to do the best.
Yeah, I guess I was a pretty good student, but I-- I couldn't further my education, due-- due to the circumstances.
I had-- I had bar mitzvah, but there was no big fuss about it.
I went to shul, and they-- and they called me to the torah, and they gave me aliyah.
And after this, they had a drink, you know, and some herring and a piece of cake.
That was the bar mitzvah.
Maybe I read-- I read part from the Torah.
Yeah, I read a part from the Torah and, you know, and that was it.
No, no party whatsoever.
There was-- there was-- there was different.
There was-- that I think they had other problems.
They were-- and mostly they didn't have the means.
Because we didn't-- it was mostly religious.
The bar mitzvah was where the boy came of age, was called to the Torah, and it was the bar mitzvah.
This-- this was it.
There was no party given, because this was not part of the ritual, at that time.
Well, it was kind of-- I-- I had a happy childhood, regardless of all the other, you know, things that we faced, being-- as a Jewish child.
It was a close-knit family, and we-- I didn't experience any, you know, like, I am being deprived of anything.
We-- was enough food in the house, and we lived in two big rooms.
I-- and the winter, you know, we didn't know anything else.
So I think I had a happy childhood.
Yeah, yeah.
My mom was a very nice person.
She-- she worked hard.
She never complained.
And-- and she had to prepare food, she had a full-time job, just to feed nine people every day.

You had breakfast and lunch and-- and, you know, and supper, and then for the holidays-- but she-- she never complained, because she had the means, she had-- we had the money to buy the food, compared to other people that problem-- had problems feeding themselves.
So in this regard, we were-- we were good off.
You know, because we didn't experience any hunger, or we didn't get all the food that we wanted to eat during the week, but there was no hunger.
And there was enough food to eat.
There was enough bread and butter and herring.
There was no, let's say, cold cuts, or other stuff, you know, but basically, there was always-- there-- there was no problem with food.
And we were kind of always busy, the family, my father had to shop in the same building, and I could always go and do something, and I was never-- I was never bored.
So they used to send you down after school to work in the shop?
That was voluntary.
That-- I was not-- I was not forced to.
But after school, I was kind of-- I felt, oh, well, brothers are working, and I-- and I was glad they let me do something, because they didn't trust me doing more.
They let me do menial things, like sweep the floor and those things.
But otherwise, you know, they didn't make me work.
Till-- till I finished school, then I started to learn the trade.
Yeah, there was-- it was four stories high, four story.
And we had-- we lived on the-- I would say on the third floor.
And the first floor, my father had the shop.
And the second floor was a synagogue, a little synagogue.
And-- and the-- and the top floor, there were, I guess, two families lived.
They were poor people, mostly.
They worked in the same room, and they lived in the same room, and everything was done in one room.
So compared to them, we had separate, so we were better off.
The time about-- about-- before the war, if I look back, mainly I think about the family and the neighbors and the Jewish life.
And-- and-- and I think a lot about it.
And it's [INAUDIBLE] I live it.
And I remember neighbors, in all those years.
I remember name.
Everything has gone out, a world disappeared.
When I when back to Poland, to, you know, take my daughter and show her where we lived, it was a very sad experience, you know, to look at the building.
And from all the people that lived there in that building, and there were children, and nobody was left for me to say hello.
So a whole world disappeared.
You know?
It just disappeared.
And it-- and this makes me very, very sad.
You know?
And I cannot stop thinking about it.
But otherwise in Poland, you know, where we lived, we were quite, you know, content, and-- because of the family life, and so forth.
We had each other.
You said that the kids always had trouble getting along with each other in school, or after school.
Not the kids.
The kids-- if it come to Polish kids, and the Jewish kids--
--there was anti-semitism.
So there was-- they were aggressive.
They used to beat us up.
But between the Jewish kids, there was no problem.
No, I was-- I was interested in between the Jewish kids and the Polish kids.
We would-- we never-- we didn't mix.
We-- we-- as the Jewish kids, we tried to stay away from them.
We had-- not-- not really friends.
But we-- we had in the same-- in the same house, the families, the Polish kids lived there, but we had no problems with them.
Always there.
Always there.
You lived it.
It was-- was getting--
--as the German-- as Hitler got more popular, more powerful, the Pollacks imitated him, you know, with the laws, and you could feel it.
In the press was there-- the press was more [? fashioned ?] after the after German hate paper.
And-- and-- in the stores they put up-- Polish stores-- Christian enterprise, you know what I mean?
So the Polish shouldn't walk in the Jewish store.
You could feel it everywhere, you know, the hate, you know, against the Jewish people, that we weren't liked, would be putting it mildly.
Oh, we had papers, and books, and--
I read.
Yeah, sure.
We knew what was going on in Germany.
We knew about the concentration camps, and so forth, and all the-- the and the-- and the hate and the propaganda.
There were-- they were always dangerous, but only getting worse.
In 1936, they had the pogrom in Lodz, not a big one, but they used to, you know, to go into Jewish homes and-- and destroy everything.
And you could feel it, that the press, the-- the-- you know, the government turned more anti-semitic.
You know, you could feel it.
We didn't talk about leaving.
We just talked about the situation, that there was-- we had no means to leave.
And we lived for so many hundreds of years.
And I was a youngster, I-- especially me, I didn't think about leaving.
If they talked about it I don't know, but we didn't have the means of-- of leaving.
They were always afraid, always talking about it's getting bad, and it's getting worse every-- every day.
But we were trapped.
You know?
He didn't have a place where to go.
So there was really no way to consider leaving.
Was no place to leaving.
We didn't have the means, and we have no-- we didn't have a place where to.
And that was home.
What time is it?
Are you going to continue for another half hour, then we have lunch?
Now--
